findings.finding: The problem statement is specific about the user group, but lacks quantitative baseline evidence for task frequency, time cost, error rate, missed doses, lost orders, or payment issues.
findings.recommendation: Add baseline metrics such as weekly orders, minutes per dose-log workflow, number of tools used, missed-dose frequency, reconstitution error incidents, and payment/order failure rate.

findings.finding: The hypothesis prescribes the solution as a unified web app rather than first stating a testable user outcome independent of implementation.
findings.recommendation: Restate the hypothesis around measurable workflow consolidation, reduced error risk, and reduced task time; keep the web app as the proposed solution.

findings.finding: The Power User persona combines end user, admin, developer, buyer, protocol manager, and operator, creating an Everything User risk.
findings.recommendation: Split the persona into at least Power User, Super Admin/Protocol Manager, and Solo Builder/Operator concerns, even if one real person currently fills all roles.

findings.finding: Compliance/legal and operational stakeholders are not represented despite ordering, health-adjacent data, managed users, and Telegram/crypto workflows.
findings.recommendation: Add stakeholder constraints for legal/compliance review, data steward/security owner, and vendor/order dependency owner.

findings.finding: The v1 scope, MoSCoW list, and phased delivery conflict. Multi-user is listed as v1/Must Have but delivered in Phase 2; data export is in Auth/Privacy but delivered in Phase 3.
findings.recommendation: Create one authoritative release-scope table showing MVP, v1, Phase 2, and deferred features, with each feature appearing in exactly one delivery phase.

findings.finding: Automated MTProto ordering is a large technical and policy dependency but is treated as core v1 scope without proving feasibility first.
findings.recommendation: Define an explicit feasibility spike or release gate for MTProto auth, send reliability, session storage, account lockout risk, and manual fallback acceptance.

findings.finding: Mobile-optimized layout is listed as Could Have while mobile dose logging is later described as first-class.
findings.recommendation: Move mobile dose-logging UX into Must Have/NFR acceptance criteria and reserve optional polish, such as dark mode, for Could Have.

findings.finding: Order cancellation, expiry, timeout, and stale vendor quote handling are explicitly missing.
findings.recommendation: Add order lifecycle states for canceled, expired, failed, abandoned, and manually completed; define who can change each state and what happens to inventory.

findings.finding: Hard gates such as zero dose-calculation defects, zero silent failures, and 100% audit log completeness are stated as absolutes but measurement scope is incomplete.
findings.recommendation: Define measurable verification methods, such as unit fixture coverage, production incident threshold, E2E failure detection criteria, and audit-event matrix coverage.

findings.finding: The ordering success target conflicts across phases: Phase 2 requires at least 20 successful orders by months 3-6, while Year 1 also requires at least 20 successful orders.
findings.recommendation: Clarify cumulative versus phase-specific targets and set realistic order-volume milestones by phase.

findings.finding: Several criteria rely on self-reporting without defining collection method or cadence.
findings.recommendation: Specify whether each metric is measured through product telemetry, in-app survey, manual journal, or admin review.

findings.finding: Monitoring and alerting requirements are missing despite hard gates around silent failures, audit completeness, backups, and Telegram send failures.
findings.recommendation: Add monitoring requirements for API errors, failed dose logs, failed Telegram sends, payment-state anomalies, audit-log write failures, backup success, and uptime alerts.

findings.finding: Data retention is only partially specified. Audit logs and backups have retention, but dose logs, order history, exports, Telegram sessions, deleted accounts, and managed-user records do not.
findings.recommendation: Add a retention matrix by data type, including deletion behavior, export availability, audit retention, backup purge timing, and Telegram session revocation.

findings.finding: Internationalization/localization requirements are absent.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly state English-only and US-only for v1, or define supported locales, units, currencies, date formats, and timezone behavior.

findings.finding: Accessibility coverage applies only to managed-user flows, excluding admin and Power User workflows.
findings.recommendation: Apply WCAG 2.1 AA to all core flows or explicitly justify any exclusions.

findings.finding: Regulatory assumptions are not validated early enough for a product that stores health-adjacent dosing data, supports managed users, and facilitates grey-market ordering.
findings.location: Section 7 Regulatory and Section 11 Risk Assessment
findings.recommendation: Move legal/regulatory review before implementation of ordering and multi-user features; document allowed claims, disclaimers, data obligations, and sourcing-module boundaries.

findings.finding: External dependency limits and costs are not documented for Telegram MTProto, transactional email, QSC catalog/PDF updates, crypto network details, hosting, and object storage.
findings.recommendation: Add dependency table with owner, API/rate limits, failure modes, cost model, credentials required, ToS risk, and fallback behavior.

findings.finding: The solo-builder constraint is documented, but the PRD scope is broad enough to create delivery risk without explicit sequencing tradeoffs.
findings.recommendation: Add capacity assumptions and a cutline for what gets removed if MTProto, multi-user, or reference content takes longer than expected.

findings.finding: Session expiry, reauthentication, and partial-save behavior are not covered for sensitive flows such as dose logging, Telegram setup, payment checklist, and account deletion.
findings.recommendation: Define behavior when a session expires mid-flow, including draft preservation, reauth prompts, and blocked destructive actions.

findings.finding: Concurrent access edge cases are missing for admin edits, managed-user dose logging, inventory updates, protocol changes, and order receipt.
findings.recommendation: Define locking, conflict resolution, idempotency, and audit behavior for simultaneous updates.

findings.finding: Dose schedule edge cases are underdefined, including missed doses, late doses, timezone changes, DST, custom frequency validation, skipped doses, and retroactive edits.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit state handling and acceptance rules for skipped, missed, late, edited, and timezone-shifted dose events.

findings.finding: Payment and order edge cases are incomplete: wrong chain/currency, duplicate tx ID, stale wallet address, vendor changes quote, partial payment, overpayment, and duplicate order sends are not defined.
findings.recommendation: Add payment validation rules, duplicate detection, quote confirmation, and order idempotency requirements.

findings.finding: Invite flow edge cases are called out as missing, including expiry and resend behavior.
findings.recommendation: Define invite expiry, resend, revoke, accepted invite, duplicate invite, and invited-but-not-active states.

findings.finding: User stories cannot be cleanly sequenced until release scope conflicts are resolved across v1, MoSCoW, and phased delivery.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the release plan before story writing; then generate epics by phase and role.

findings.finding: Several features are too broad for story decomposition without additional acceptance rules, including adherence dashboard, reference profile quality, protocol assignment, stack overview, and audit completeness.
findings.recommendation: For each feature, add role, trigger, required fields, system behavior, empty state, error state, permissions, and acceptance criteria.

findings.finding: Permissions are partially defined but not complete enough for story-level acceptance tests across Power User, managed user, and account deletion/export flows.
findings.recommendation: Add a permission matrix covering create/read/update/delete rights for protocols, dose logs, reconstitution records, orders, exports, managed accounts, and audit logs.

handoff_notes: Do not proceed directly to User Stories. First resolve the P0 scope conflicts, legal/regulatory gating, and release sequencing. Then create an epic map by persona and phase, plus matrices for permissions, data retention, external dependencies, order lifecycle states, and dose/event edge cases. Once those are stable, stories can be written with much less guesswork.
